evidence: Multiple subsystems use layered runtime projection wrappers that mostly remap callbacks without adding domain policy (for example importer runtime scope projections and per-scope resolvers in goseg/importer/importer.go:307-407, and bootstrap/c2c runtime context wrappers in goseg/main.go:58-131 and :155-260).

Global singleton defaults are used as hidden dependency sources across event/broadcast/orchestration flows (goseg/broadcast/state.go:17-18, :98-114; goseg/broadcast/loop.go:44-45, :108-140; goseg/docker/events/events.go:17, :34-40), reducing interface honesty because call sites appear runtime-injectable but still depend on process-global state.

Config access is frequently carried via broad snapshot bags and then narrowed downstream (goseg/config/config.go:77-90, :181-200; goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:227-239; goseg/importer/importer.go:105, :280-289), which weakens abstraction leverage by coupling workflows to wider config domains than required.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large mixed directories for this scope: goseg/config/ has 41 files and goseg/system/ has 39 files, each combining multiple concerns under one package boundary.

goseg/system/disk_fstab.go and goseg/system/disk_health.go are parent-package pass-through files that delegate entirely into subpackages (groundseg/system/disk and groundseg/system/storage), indicating boundary overlap rather than clear ownership.

goseg/config/bootstrap.go contains process bootstrap and environment/runtime wiring (initOnce, GS_BASE_PATH, os.Args debug parsing, path initialization), while goseg/config/config.go also defines runtime context defaults and setters; startup/runtime concerns are split across config internals instead of a dedicated startup boundary.

goseg/shipworkflow/ is another overloaded directory in this batch context (49 files in directory_profiles), and goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go mixes reusable polling/stop-wait helpers with ship config persistence seam logic in a top-level workflow package.

Global mutable runtime seams are repeatedly reset per-test (`resetExporterState`, `resetUploadSessionsForTest`, `resetLeakStateForTest`, `resetLoggerInitForTest`), indicating behavior depends on package-level state rather than scoped test/runtime objects.

Core runtime files combine multiple concern layers in single units: `accesspoint.go` mixes config/default resolution, process probing/matching, shell execution, and lifecycle entrypoints; `urbit_operations_runtime.go` mixes transition registry, config mutation orchestration, external service calls, and post-transition broadcasting.

Configuration handling remains structurally broad and repetitive: `structs.SysConfig` and section update APIs are duplicated by section, while tests in `config_test.go` require very large hand-built config fixtures to exercise merge logic.

`config` exposes both `Config()` and `Conf()` for the same behavior, while related types also mix `Conf*` and `Config*` prefixes (`ConfPatch`, `ConfUpdateOption`), creating avoidable vocabulary split for the same domain concept.

Long-running loop workers use inconsistent lifecycle naming: `OldLogsCleanerWithContext` is a perpetual ticker loop, while similar patterns are named with explicit `Loop`/`Start*` semantics (`CheckVersionLoopWithContext`, `StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`).

Cross-module callback seam names overuse generic `Fn` suffixes and runtime labels (`StartContainerFn`, `GetStartramServicesFn`, `InitializeConfigFn`, `RunImportedPierWorkflowFn`), reducing intent clarity and making domain behavior harder to scan quickly.

findings.summary: Scope-specific runtime projection layers add indirection cost without enough behavioral leverage.
evidence: Importer defines composite runtime, then repeatedly projects it into init/workflow/publish variants with separate resolver paths (initRuntime/workflowRuntime/publishRuntime and resolveImporterRuntimeFor* chain in goseg/importer/importer.go:307-407).
main bootstrap and C2C paths add wrapper methods that mostly nil-check and forward to function fields (goseg/main.go:99-131 and :215-260).

Startup runtime repeats similar callback-carrying structures for init/background phases, with many fields passed through to spec tables (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:88-117, :245-297).
findings.suggestion: Consolidate runtime seams around smaller behavior-focused interfaces (for example `ImporterStorage`, `ImporterProvision`, `StartupInitDeps`) and remove projection methods that only copy fields. Keep one resolver per behavior boundary, and let call sites depend on narrow interfaces directly.

findings.summary: Global singleton fallbacks undermine explicit dependency injection contracts.
evidence: Broadcast state and loop maintain global defaults (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastLoopController`) and public entrypoints call them implicitly (goseg/broadcast/state.go:17-18, :108-114; goseg/broadcast/loop.go:44-45, :104-110, :137-155).
Event runtime uses a package-level singleton via `sync.OnceValue` and many call paths use `DefaultEventRuntime()` rather than explicit broker wiring (goseg/docker/events/events.go:17, :34-40).

Docker subsystem startup/stop wrappers normalize nil runtime to package default, introducing hidden shared state in code that appears parameterized (goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:52-57, :94-106, :141-155).
findings.suggestion: Make singleton usage explicit at composition roots only. Convert package entrypoints to require runtime/broker parameters (or a constructor-produced service object), and keep legacy globals only in compatibility shims.

findings.summary: Config snapshot bag abstractions are wider than consumer needs, increasing cross-domain coupling.
evidence: Snapshot DTOs group multiple domains (`ImportSettings`, `StartupSettings`, `C2CSettings`) and are passed through runtime seams even when only one subsection is required (goseg/config/config.go:77-90, :181-200).
Importer init workflow consumes `ImportSettings` then immediately narrows to swap path fields (goseg/importer/importer.go:105, :280-289), indicating bag width exceeds use-site needs.

Startup and main runtime contexts expose composite settings and then peel nested subfields (`settings.Update`, `settings.Startram`, `settings.Runtime`, `settings.Connectivity`) rather than requesting only necessary values (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:227-239; goseg/main.go:155-170, :225-246).
findings.suggestion: Replace bag snapshots in seams with minimal query functions (for example `SwapSettingsFn`, `ConnectivityIntervalFn`, `IsDebugModeFn`) or narrow typed views per subsystem. Keep wide snapshots only for serialization/reporting boundaries.

findings.summary: System-level disk files are bridge wrappers over subpackages, creating overlapping ownership boundaries.
evidence: disk_fstab.go defines parse/reconcile/read/write helpers that directly call systemdisk.ParseFstabLine/ReconcileFstabLines/ReadFstabLines/WriteFstabLines without local policy.
disk_health.go exports SmartResultsSnapshot/SetSmartResults/ListHardDisks/IsDevMounted/SmartCheckAllDrives and forwards directly to storage package functions.
The test file disk_fstab_test.go is a smoke placeholder, which reinforces that this file is currently acting as a placement/compatibility shim rather than an owned domain unit.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create explicit compatibility boundary package `goseg/system/legacydisk` and move bridge files there (`git mv goseg/system/disk_fstab.go goseg/system/legacydisk/fstab_bridge.go` and `git mv goseg/system/disk_health.go goseg/system/legacydisk/health_bridge.go`). (2) Update callers to import `goseg/system/disk` or `goseg/system/storage` directly where possible; keep temporary bridge exports only for external API stability. (3) Remove placeholder smoke tests and add package-specific tests in destination packages. (4) Validate imports/build with `go test ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/routines/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Bootstrap/runtime process concerns are embedded in config package files instead of a dedicated startup boundary.
evidence: bootstrap.go manages process initialization state (`initOnce`, global readiness, path setup, debug mode argument parsing from os.Args, environment-based base path) together with config loading.

config.go also owns runtime context defaults and mutation (`basePathDefault`, `architectureDefault`, `SetBasePath`, `SetDebugMode`, `SetDockerDir`), further mixing process boot/runtime concerns inside config package.
directory_profiles marks goseg/config/ as a high-count mixed directory (41 files), consistent with flattened concerns and weaker navigability.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Introduce `goseg/startup/runtimectx` for runtime context defaults/setters and `goseg/startup/bootstrap` for initialization orchestration. (2) Move functions in order: first context primitives from `config.go`, then bootstrap orchestration from `bootstrap.go`, then update config package to consume those APIs. (3) Keep temporary forwarding wrappers in `config` only during migration. (4) Run import-update and validation commands: `go test ./goseg/config/... ./goseg/startup...` then full `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Generic lifecycle polling helpers are placed in a large workflow package, increasing package breadth and weakening layering.
evidence: lifecycle.go defines generic-style helpers (`PollWithTimeout`, `WaitForUrbitStopWith`, `PersistUrbitConfig`) that are reusable lifecycle primitives but live in a broad top-level workflow package.
doc.go describes shipworkflow as coordinating new-ship, StarTram, and Urbit operations, indicating already broad package scope.
directory_profiles shows goseg/shipworkflow/ at 49 files, a flat and mixed directory where additional generic helpers reduce navigability and ownership clarity.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create focused package `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle` (or `goseg/internal/transitionlifecycle` if internal-only) for polling/stop lifecycle primitives. (2) Move `lifecycle.go` first, then update all shipworkflow callers/imports to use new package APIs, then keep short deprecated forwarding funcs only if needed. (3) Replace placeholder lifecycle smoke test with behavior tests in the destination package. (4) Validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/internal/transitionlifecycle/...` and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Protocol and StarTram contract families are colocated in one flat contracts package, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` defines websocket protocol action contracts and governance owners like `uploadsvc`/`system/wifi`.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` defines StarTram error contracts (`APIConnectionError`) in the same package despite StarTram having its own top-level domain package and governance doc reference in `README.md`.
Packet directory profile marks `goseg/protocol/contracts/` as 11 files, indicating flat-folder overload where mixed contract families now share one location.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol/` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram/` plus a small root `registry.go` facade in `goseg/protocol/contracts`; (2) move `protocol_contracts.go` first into `.../protocol/`, update internal references, then move `startram_contracts.go` into `.../startram/`; (3) keep exported access through root facade functions to avoid immediate downstream churn; (4) run import/validation commands: `cd goseg && gofmt -w protocol/contracts && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./protocol/conformance/... ./startram/...` and finally `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Canonical action tokens are split across micro-packages, creating a fragmented change boundary for protocol contract updates.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` contains only string constants (namespace/actions), but `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` immediately re-wraps those constants into contract-facing types and IDs.

`goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go` verifies fixture coverage via `protocol/actions` constants, meaning one protocol action change spans at least three locations/packages to confirm full consistency.
This distribution makes ownership/navigation unclear for contributors trying to modify action naming or compatibility metadata in one pass.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) establish a single target vocabulary package, e.g. `goseg/protocol/actionspec/`, that owns namespace/action tokens plus typed aliases currently split between `actionids` and contracts; (2) move constants from `actionids` first and add temporary type aliases/shims in old package to keep callers compiling; (3) update `protocol_contracts.go` and conformance fixtures to consume `actionspec` directly, then remove shim usage; (4) run import/validation commands: `cd goseg && gofmt -w protocol/actionids protocol/contracts protocol/conformance && go test ./protocol/actions/... ./protocol/contracts/... ./protocol/conformance/... ./uploadsvc/...` followed by `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Test design relies on shared package-global mutation across modules, creating systemic coupling and weak isolation boundaries.
evidence: `resetExporterState` rewires package globals (`mkdirAllForExporter`, `getStoragePathForExporter`, `openForExporter`, `whitelist`) for each test.
`resetUploadSessionsForTest` mutates global `uploadSessions` under `uploadMu` and restores via cleanup.
`resetLeakStateForTest` swaps global `BytesChan` and `lickStatuses`, restoring later, and multiple tests depend on this side effect.
`resetLoggerInitForTest` mutates global logger init state (`setLoggerInitState`, `setLoggerInitErr`) for test behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit runtime/store instances per subsystem (exporter, importer, leak, logger) and pass them through constructors/handlers; keep one compatibility default for production, but have tests instantiate isolated runtimes instead of mutating globals.

findings.summary: Operational orchestration files mix unrelated responsibilities, reducing structural focus and increasing change blast radius.
evidence: `accesspoint.go` contains runtime seam wiring, config/default normalization, password derivation, process matching/parsing, dependency checks, and shell execution in one file.

`urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains command registry mapping, transition step construction, config mutation orchestration (`runShipConfigTransition`), external side effects (docker/click/startram), and schedule publishing logic.
`subsystem_events.go` combines health-loop lifecycle control, event listener transport wiring, event-bus backpressure policy, and transition execution/retry-window policy.
findings.suggestion: Split each file into role-focused modules: runtime wiring/defaults, domain transition planning, and side-effect executors; define narrow coordinators that compose these parts so policy changes do not require touching transport/parsing/process code together.

findings.summary: Config modeling remains monolithic, which drives repetitive per-field setup and duplicated section-level update scaffolding.
evidence: `structs/configs.go` repeats near-identical section accessor/update methods (`ConnectivitySection`, `StartramSection`, etc. and corresponding `Update*Config`).

`config_test.go` builds very large default/custom config fixtures field-by-field in multiple tests (e.g., migration and override tests), indicating missing reusable builders/fixtures and high schema coupling in tests.
`config_update_schema.go` maintains key-to-parser/apply registries and specialized parse helpers, which amplifies maintenance when schema evolves.
findings.suggestion: Introduce canonical config fixture builders per section and table-driven patch cases; consolidate section update/access patterns via generic helpers or a section registry to reduce manual repetition when fields evolve.

findings.summary: Config APIs mix `Conf` and `Config` names for the same concept, causing naming drift.
evidence: `goseg/config/bootstrap.go` defines both `Config()` and alias `Conf()` for identical behavior.
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` centers patch structures on `ConfPatch` while other package APIs use `Config` terminology.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` uses `ConfUpdateOption`/`UpdateConfTypedFn`, reinforcing mixed terminology outside `config`.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on `Config` naming for all public and internal symbols (for example `ConfPatch` -> `ConfigPatch`, `UpdateConfTypedFn` -> `UpdateConfigTypedFn`), keep temporary aliases only for compatibility, and add a deprecation window with targeted call-site migration.

findings.summary: Some perpetual background loops are named like one-shot cleaners instead of loop services.
evidence: `OldLogsCleanerWithContext` runs an infinite ticker loop until context cancellation, but the name reads like a one-time cleanup action.
`startup_orchestrator` wires this as a background service (`StartOldLogsCleanerFn`), confirming daemon-like lifecycle rather than single cleanup call.
Nearby routines use clearer lifecycle names (`CheckVersionLoopWithContext`, `StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`), showing inconsistent semantics for similar behavior.
findings.suggestion: Rename looping workers to explicit lifecycle names (for example `RunOldLogsCleanupLoop` / `StartOldLogsCleanupLoop`) and keep thin deprecated wrappers to preserve compatibility while call sites migrate.

findings.summary: Widespread `*Fn` callback naming obscures domain intent and creates low-signal APIs.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` exposes dense callback fields like `StartContainerFn`, `GetShipStatusFn`, `LoadMCFn`, `SvcDeleteFn` with mixed abbreviation quality and little behavioral intent in names.
`startup_orchestrator.go` follows the same pattern (`InitializeConfigFn`, `StartMDNSServerFn`, `StartOldLogsCleanerFn`), adding repetition of generic prefixes across many seams.
`importer.go` continues the pattern (`RunImportedPierWorkflowFn`, `ShipworkflowWaitForBootCodeFn`, `PublishImportTransitionFn`), making seams verbose but still semantically thin.
findings.suggestion: Define a naming convention that encodes action semantics before seam type (for example `StartContainer`, `WaitForBootCode`, `PublishImportTransition`) and reserve `Fn` suffix only for private local variables; refactor runtime structs in batches with compatibility aliases where needed.
